summary:
Central Retinal Arterial Occlusion usually does not lead to direct optic atrophy; it may result from extended ischemia rather than an inherent feature of the condition itself. Other diseases such as glaucoma often do present with optic atrophy because they involve sustained pressure-related injury or toxic insults affecting the optic nerve head more definitively. Therefore,
Central Retinal Artery Occlusion
is a plausible exclusion for causing primary optic atrophy immediately post-event. To be precise within this specific scenario where only one option fits best based upon typical presentations and pathophysiology described here today during our discussion session regarding
